data.title: ROBO: Bet On The Consumers, Not The Innovators
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Robo Global Robotics and Automation Index ETF (NYSEARCA:ROBO) unfortunately seems too diversified to deliver outperformance. Based on the historical performance, the ETF failed to beat the SP 500 over a one, three, and five year time frame. It seems like a simple index fund would do a better job of giving investors exposure to innovative, strong companies that are likely to benefit from automation. Ultimately, I believe the companies that use automation and robotics to improve productivity are the major beneficiaries, not the companies that produce the automation and robotics solutions. Therefore, I rate this ETF as an avoid as it is too diversified, with many of its holdings seemingly fully priced.

The Customer Usually Wins

According to the ETF's official website description,

The ROBO Global Robotics & Automation Index ETF invests in global companies that are driving transformative innovations in robotics, automation, and artificial intelligence, including companies that create technology to enable truly intelligent systems that can sense, process, and act, and companies that apply those technologies to deliver RAAI-enabled products—including robots—to businesses and consumers.

Sounds riveting. However, my opinion is that this industry is brutally competitive, and that the ultimate beneficiary are not these companies but the customer. In other words, the users of this technology are likely to win, not the creators.

When the dot-com frenzy began in the late 90s, many new internet companies had great growth prospects but no clear path to profitability. A land-grab ensued, with companies like NetScape, MySpace, and Pets.com to name a few. Many thought that these companies could grow their business with users, but few actually pondered the true profitability potential of these names. Eventually, when the dot com bubble burst, it became clear that obvious growth did not lead to obvious profits. In the end, the real winners in aggregate weren't the internet companies, but rather us, the users of the internet.

I think we are seeing a similar story here. To me, the real winners in aggregate aren't the companies that create automation technology, but rather the companies that use automation to improve their productivity. It's the consumers that use RAAI-enabled products that win in the aggregate, as they benefit from massively improved productivity and labor cost savings to enhance operational efficiency.

To support my claim that most of these companies don't capture most of the value they create, we can see that some of the ETF's holdings have relatively low profit margins, suggesting that they don't gain that much from offering automation solutions.

Some of these companies in this ETF don't actually make much money, and in some cases still lose money. My take is that some of these companies are unable to capture much of the value they create, as most of the benefit goes to the consumer. These benefits range from automating repetitive workflow, advanced data and market analysis, and enhancing product features. I think most of the R&D and investments these companies make serve to benefit the customer, and not their own bottom lines.

Finally, the ETF seems too diversified to deliver outperformance. With 90 holdings, I think it would be better if they concentrated a little more on promising companies with cheaper valuations and strong growth prospects. The track record agrees with me so far, with this ETF lagging the SP 500 index pretty dramatically since inception.

While the companies in the ROBO ETF may create a lot of value, I think it's the consumers that will capture the majority of that value. The past track record seems to agree with me so far, and the bottom line is that this ETF is both too diversified and unprepared to capture the value that it creates.

Holdings Seem Fairly Priced

According to the ETF's website, the top 5 holdings currently are:

Some of these companies are rather expensive from a valuation multiple perspective. To me, they don't seem to be trading at bargain prices and are already correctly priced, giving investors limited upside from current valuations.

Based on this chart, the valuations for the top 5 holdings except for Nvidia (NVDA) are quite stable. The market seems to have gotten comfortable pricing these companies based on their sales at a stable P/S ratio. While many holdings show earnings, most are already priced at pretty high P/E ratios that already account for their superior growth prospects.

The bottom line is that I believe this ETF has limited upside for investors, with many of its holdings correctly priced based on their valuation multiples. Now that interest rates are no longer near zero, many of these holdings with elevated valuations may be vulnerable to market corrections, and the ETF's risk doesn't seem to be worth the reward.

Unions May Oppose Automation

Recently, the dockworkers strike on October 2024 suggested that some unions and workers reject the idea of automation and robotics in their jobs. According to the union's demands for the dockworkers in the news,

The dockworkers striking up and down the East Coast are, culturally and geographically, a world apart from the Hollywood actors and writers who staged a four-month walkout last year. But their protests share a common core principle: They don’t want bots taking their jobs.

The way this ETF is setup, it is built on the overall success of automating work so that people don't have to do it manually anymore. To me, the unions make no sense as they should be in support of the automation trend to increase their real wages. An increase in real wages can only be supported by an increase in productivity, and the use of robots and automation in their jobs should be a net benefit to their productivity.

In any case, the unions who want to protect their jobs may oppose this automation trend, presenting a headwind for this ETF. Many of the companies in this ETF are innovating robotics and automation solutions that may see pushback from regulators, unions, and other manual workers. I think we have yet to see the full regulatory framework emerge yet, presenting a potential headwind to the companies in this ETF. The bottom line is that the combination of hostile unions and red tape may stall progress and growth prospects for many of the companies in this ETF.

Risks

I don't think this ETF is poised to deliver outperformance. The price refuses to breakout past $60, and the vast majority of the holdings seem fully priced. However, I could be wrong if excitement and new technologies attract more investment attention and cause more money to flow into ETFs like ROBO.

Perhaps it's different this time. The real winners in aggregate could be the companies in the ETF portfolio, in which case my thesis could be invalid. While I believe the main beneficiaries are the consumers, it could be that the innovators that create the technology benefit more than the users. In that case, this ETF could see more upside.

Avoid ROBO

If investors really wanted to benefit from the robotics and automation revolution, a simple SP 500 index fund should suffice. This is because I think the main beneficiary of this trend aren't the creators, but the users. Many of the Fortune 500 companies in the index fund are the true beneficiaries, and will capture most of the value that the ROBO ETF companies create. They can automate their workforce, increase productivity efficiency, and adapt to this new automation revolution to boost their bottom lines.

data.title: Lululemon Has Started A Recovery (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU)(TSX:LLL:CA) appears ready to continue to move across its wide trading range for a gain of at least 20%. The technical factors that indicate this are its long-term relative strength (RSI) and a level of bearish expectation for LULU that has historically occurred just before major advances. Only time will tell whether this move will be the start of a new bull trend.

Lululemon Has Been In A Four-Year Trading Range

The chart below shows the closing price of Lululemon since 2018 and the sideways trend channel that’s contained its price movement for four years. The stock’s made zero gain since the start of COVID in February 2020. The width of the channel is either .50% or 100% depending on which line you’re measuring from.

We believe the $233 July low marked the start of another move across the range. Our minimum price target is $350, which is the middle of the range. At its current price of $287 that would be a further gain of 22%.

Investor Expectations For LULU Are “Too Negative”

You want to avoid a stock when "too many" investors think the price is going higher, and buy it when "too many" think the price is going lower. The indicator shown below helps an investor do this. It measures investor expectations. Green zone readings occur when "too many" investors expect lower prices, while red zone readings occur when "too many" investors expect higher prices.

We've used green arrows to indicate greens on readings in Lulu in the past, and they occurred just before or near intermediate term price rises. The overall decline this year in LULU was accompanied by three clear Green Zone readings; the most recent being during the small price rally of the last few weeks. This level of negativity in the face of a price rise is generally a sign that investors don't think this rally will last. It’s the type of disbelief we like to see, and it usually occurs before a major, intermediate term advance.

Long-Term Relative Strength of LULU Is Positive

This last chart shows the long-term relative strength index for LULU. Most technicians simply use the default parameters for RSI set by the technical website service they use. These are always short term. We calculate our RSI indicator to measure long-term relative strength; the chart below is not a short-term view.

It shows that in July, the long-term RSI reached the same level of being oversold as occurred at the major low during the COVID bear market in 2020. We’ve indicated this with two black arrows. In fact, the low this July went even deeper than the 2020 low. This is a major technical sign that July marked the start of a strong intermediate to long-term advance.

Conclusion

Two powerful technical signals indicate LULU made a major intermediate to long-term price low in July and has started a move back up across its large trading range. A conservative estimate is to move halfway, which would be a price target of $355. We think intermediate-term traders in LULU can safely enter with expectations of a gain of 20% or more. We believe current long-term investors in LULU should hold their position and reassess their situation once this price target is achieved.

data.title: Karooooo: An Overlooked GARP Gem
data.description:
This article emphasizes Karooooo Ltd. (NASDAQ:KARO) as we assess an overlooked asset with growth-at-a-reasonable-price attributes.

For those unaware, Karooooo is a global mobility company that offers software-as-a-service. The company has experienced numerous material events lately, ranging from a second-quarter earnings release to insider stock sales amendments and a volatile stock price, providing us with the necessary latitude to assess its stock.

Without further ado, here's our take on Karooooo's prospects.

Structure And Key Value Drivers

Business Structure

Karooooo offers two major products: Cartrack and Logistical Services. The former is a security management system that tracks vehicles to ensure safety and recoverability, whereas the latter extends beyond Cartrack to provide logistical management software.

The following diagram dispenses the company's business line and geographic revenue mix. Karooooo's Cartrack is focused on South Africa's market, but yields revenue from other regions, such as APAC and EMEA nations.

The company's Cartrack offering delivers much higher profit margins than its logistics and Carzuka (soon to be closed) segments. However, the logistics segment remains at an early stage, whereas Cartrack is a mature business with a substantial presence, meaning a direct comparison might be misleading.

In a nutshell, the company's core business and cash cow are Cartrack. Yet, we believe its logistics segment provides cross-sales synergies while delivering a new growth opportunity, especially through foreign market exposure.

Value Drivers

Karooooo has a few identifiable value drivers. Let's start with Cartrack's value drivers. The business line has shown comprehensive uptake through its subscription model, a sales method that we consider smoother than pure product sales.

Being a South African, I can tell you that vehicle safety is a significant concern, as both light vehicles and trucks have suffered from rising hijackings. The region's safety issues place it in the World Population Review's top ten on index. Despite the negativity of crime, Karooooo's solutions add a positive effect by offering vehicle and fleet owners the necessary tools to protect themselves. Sure, the company profits from its offerings, but it is a win-win situation for it and its customers.

Low unemployment rates, political corruption, and insufficient policing budgets mean demand for vehicle safety tools like Cartrack will likely grow until structural changes occur.

Reverting to Karooooo's broad-based earnings shows that its logistics unit remains a work in progress. However, the segment has shown scintillating growth.

Karooooo's logistics segment revenue increased by 40% year-over-year to R101 million (approximately $5.75 million). The segment's growth rate outpaced Cartrack's by 2.66x, showing its prospects as a catalyst for broad-based expansion.

We think Karooooo's logistics segment has international growth prospects. Cartrack's growth is concentrated, primarily relying on unsafe operating environments, whereas the logistics segment suits any geographic environment. Moreover, the segment benefits from a lean operating model, allowing consumers to scale their operations without investing large sums of capital in internal assets.

Herewith is an overview of Karooooo's logistics segment.

Reviewing Karooooo's Finances

Q2 Earnings Results

Karooooo released its second-quarter earnings report on October 15, revealing a GAAP earnings-per-share beat of one cent and a revenue miss of $853 900. The market reacted strongly, leading to a 15.6% drawdown in its American Depositary Receipts.

Here's a snapshot of Karooooo in pre-market on October 16th.

Despite missing its revenue target, Karooooo's subscription revenue grew by 15% year-over-year to ZAR986 million (approximately $56.16 million), driven by a 17% increase in subscribers. In addition, Karooooo's earnings per share increased by a phenomenal 31% year-over-year, showing signs of margin expansion.

The company maintained its guidance at the midpoint, and despite Karooooo's revenue miss, its real growth remains robust. Therefore, we believe investors have overreacted to its revenue miss.

Balance Sheet and Cash Flows

Karooooo's balance sheet has softened year-over-year. However, it maintains what we consider a solid debtor's days and notable bank deposits, allowing it to maintain its short-term liquidity.

The company's balance sheet is supported by free cash flow growth, which it achieved while realizing outflows from investing activities via an R316 million (about $17.94 million) investment in developing its South African central office.

We think Karooooo remains in good standing, allowing it to reinvest in its business and deliver dividends to its shareholders.

Recent News

Karooooo Refrains From Secondary Offering

Jose Calisto, Karooooo’s CEO, triggered an announced secondary offering in July, allowing the sale of shares worth $75 million in gross proceeds. To our knowledge, Karooooo wouldn't have raised capital through the sale. Instead, it was merely a shareholder seeking divestment.

As you might've gathered, the secondary offer was canceled as the selling shareholder deemed the sale unfavorable. However, this doesn't discount a future sale, as the selling shareholder cited market conditions.

We believe a sale of this magnitude might be executed at a discount, dragging Karooooo's stock price downward. Nevertheless, the CEO's unwillingness to sell might be interpreted as a positive from a company performance standpoint (Karooooo's shares jumped by 7.6% after the announcement).

Wall Street Price Targets

Karooooo received three ratings from Wall Street in the past 90 days. Seeking Alpha's database shows that the stock maintains two buy and one strong buy ratings, with an average price target of $43.58 per share. Although Wall Street ratings can be inaccurate, they often provide positive sentiment-based support as various market participants "buy into" Wall Street's research.

Valuation and Dividend Metrics

A table outlining some of Karooooo's key price multiples is shown below; a discussion follows.

Metric Value
PEG 0.72x
EV/EBITDA 12.26x
Price-to-Cash Flow 15.36x

Source: Seeking Alpha

The stock's price-to-earnings-growth multiple sits below one, suggesting Karooooo's earnings-per-share growth has outpaced its stock price momentum. This observation is particularly important as the stock's non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 25.4x is 9.34% above its five-year average and 5.18% above the sector median.

Furthermore, Karooooo has an EV/EBITDA ratio of 12.26, which is below the sector median of 19.32x. Although the metric is 9.08% above Karooooo's five-year average, the company's rapid growth and margin expansion likely justify an inflated ratio.

Karooooo's price-to-cash flow ratio of 15.36x is below the sector median of 19.65x. Like its EV/EBITDA, Karooooo's P/CF ratio exceeds its five-year average (by 9.08%). As previously shown, the company's cash flows decreased in Q2. However, much of the slowdown can be attributed to an investing outflow.

Finally, Karooooo has a three-year dividend yield on cost of 3.27% and a four-year average dividend yield of 1.84%. We find Karooooo's dividend highly compelling for a growth phase stock.

Risk Factors

Concentration Risk

Karooooo has numerous identifiable risks. Having said that, we consider its primary risk to be operational concentration. The company's excess exposure to South Africa may introduce cyclicality, as much of Karooooo's revenue mix relies on a single economy.

Although it is common for companies to operate in single economies, South Africa is a struggling economy with cyclical attributes. As such, we deem concentration risk a potential overhang.

Some Valuation Metrics Are Questionable

A few price multiples were dispensed in the previous section. We remain bullish about Karooooo's fundamental valuation outlook. Still, some of its price multiples are above their normalized averages (five-year averages). Although the company's growth might justify such valuations, some investors might see things differently, especially as its stock is above its 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages.

Closing Thoughts

Karooooo's second-quarter revenue miss spooked the market. However, its headline growth remains exponential, suggesting market participants have overreacted.

Despite likely facing concentration risk and normalized valuation inefficiencies, the company's price-to-earnings ratio conveys Karooooo's fundamental stealth, which derives from noteworthy representation in overlooked SaaS mobility markets.

We think Karooooo is a strong buy.

data.title: Charles Schwab: Solid Investment Setup
data.description:
Shares of brokerage powerhouse Charles Schwab (NYSE:SCHW) were up more than 6% after the company reported better than expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter. The financial services company saw robust top line momentum and strong asset gathering capabilities through the quarter that implies attractive potential for earnings growth. I believe the risk profile has further improved here, and I see upside revaluation potential if the brokerage platform continues to grow its core net new assets. I am rating shares of Charles Schwab a strong buy and see a fair value of $90/share!

Previous ratings

I recommended Charles Schwab aggressively in July after the brokerage firm submitted its second fiscal quarter earnings scorecard in July: This Is A Golden Buying Opportunity. I recommended the financial services company chiefly because of its ability to attract core net new assets to its platform. Additionally, Charles Schwab is highly profitable, which the company’s third-quarter earnings sheet confirmed yet again. I believe shares have revaluation potential, and I am not yet ready to sell.

Better than expected earnings from Charles Schwab in Q3’24

Charles Schwab reported better than expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter, with the financial brokerage earning $0.77 per share, showing a $0.02/share beat. The top line came in at $4.85B, which beat the average prediction by $61M.

Charles Schwab generated $4.8B in net revenues in the third fiscal quarter, showing 5% year-over-year. This growth was driven in part by robust market activity and stock market indices climbing to fresh highs lately. As a major trading platform, Charles Schwab benefits from investors trading in and out of positions in order to take advantage of market volatility.

In the third-quarter, the financial services company also generated $1.41B in earnings, showing a solid 25% year-over-year earnings growth rate. This growth, in part, is due to the brokerage cutting operating expenditures, which has had a positive earnings impact in the third fiscal quarter. As a result, Charles Schwab’s profit margin expanded 8 PP year-over-year to a massive 38% in Q3'24, driven by cost cuts as well as earnings growth that is related to Charles Schwab attracting more client assets to its platform.

Charles Schwab’s client assets grew to $9.92T in Q3'24, showing a massive 27% year-over-year growth. Further, the financial services platform continued to attract a ton of core net new assets, a measure that excludes inflows of new assets related to acquisitions: in Q3’24, Charles Schwab’s core net new assets amounted to $95.3B, showing 109% growth year-over-year.

Zooming out a little bit, we can see that strength in attracting core net new assets was not a singular event: the financial services platform consistently managed to attract assets throughout the year. In the first nine months of FY 2024, Charles Schwab attained $252B in core net new assets, showing 10% year-over-year growth. The brokerage's ability to consistently gather assets from clients -- which are then being put to use on the Charles Schwab platform and which generate fees in return -- is one of the most endearing qualities of an investment in SCHW.

Charles Schwab’s valuation

Charles Schwab, despite a solid 16% gain since my last recommendation in July, is not yet overpriced. The financial services company is expected to earn $3.80 per-share in FY 2025 and estimates have broadly fallen lately, in part due to expectations of weaker net interest margins in a lower-rate world. However, Charles Schwab is undervalued relative to its historical average P/E ratio as well as the valuation ratio of its biggest rival, Interactive Brokers (IBKR).

If Charles Schwab revalues to its 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio of 20.85X, then shares have at least 10% upside potential. The average P/E ratio of 20.85X implies a fair value of $80 per-share. If Charles Schwab would achieve the same valuation level as Interactive Brokers -- which also runs a highly profitable trading platform -- then shares could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $87 per-share.

A revaluation catalyst, in my opinion, lies in the fact that the company is attracting a ton of new assets which will generate fees for Charles Schwab going forward. The brokerage also has a strong history of growing core net new assets consistently, so Charles Schwab is not seeing a whole lot of volatility in its inflows. A higher asset base translates into stronger EPS growth potential going forward, which also points at improving platform profitability.

Risks with Charles Schwab

Charles Schwab is set for long-term asset and earnings growth and looks to be in great financial shape from a profitability perspective. As a trading-focused trading platform, Charles Schwab benefits from volatility which tends to entice customers to engage in more transactions, benefiting the top and bottom line of the financial services company. What would change my mind about Charles Schwab is if the firm were to see falling profitability, lower margins, and a decline in its earnings growth rate.

Final thoughts

Charles Schwab submitted a strong earnings sheet for the third fiscal quarter that once again showed very robust earnings growth and high platform profitability. Yet, shares of the financial brokerage company are still very reasonably valued and currently trade at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of only 18.9X, well below the longer-term average price-to-earnings ratio. With Charles Schwab also yet again demonstrating that it can grow its client assets and attract billions of new core net new assets throughout the quarter, the financial services platform is facing improved earnings growth prospects. In my opinion, shares have an attractive risk profile and not yet overpriced, despite a positive market reaction after the release of Q3'24 earnings. Since Charles Schwab is a general beneficiary of higher market volatility (which translates to higher transaction-related revenues), I believe the brokerage is a solid long-term investment for investors that want to gain exposure to the financial services industry.

data.title: FAX: Modest Rebound In Performance But Still An Avoid
data.description:
It has been a while since I last analyzed the abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund Inc (NYSE:FAX), so I was quite surprised when I recently came across the ticker and the share price had jumped to almost $17 / share. From what I can recall, the FAX fund was trading near $3 / share, so did something happen in the past year that massively boosted the fund's returns?

Unfortunately, upon closer examination, the FAX fund's large jump in share price was simply the result of a 1-for-6 reverse stock split, effected on September 9, 2024. Reverse stock splits may boost share prices, but have no impact on investors' total returns, since the number of shares an investor owns is reduced.

On a total return basis, the FAX fund has done modestly well, effectively covering its distribution, as evidenced by the split-adjusted share price rising by 3% since May 2023 (Figure 1). This has translated into total returns of 25.5% in the past year and a half.

With improved performance, is the FAX fund now worthy of an upgrade?

In my opinion, the FAX fund has benefited from an easing of global interest rates, allowing FAX's portfolio to generate price gains. However, longer term, I do not foresee FAX's portfolio being able to sustain the 10% average annual return necessary to fund its distribution. I continue to recommend investors avoid the FAX fund.

Brief Fund Overview

The abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund, Inc. is an income-oriented closed-end fund ("CEF") that primarily invests in Asian debt securities. The FAX fund has $764 million in net assets as of September 30, 2024 and charged a 1.29% net expense ratio (Figure 2).

Readers should note that the net expense ratio stated above only refers to the fund's management and other expenses. Including interest expense, the FAX fund's total expense ratio was an eye-watering 3.33% for Fiscal 2023 (Figure 3).

Reduced Portfolio Leverage

Since the last time I looked at FAX's portfolio (based on FAX's December 31, 2022 factsheet), the fund has noticeably shifted its portfolio allocations. Geographically, the FAX fund has reduced exposure to India (25.4% to 16.5%), Indonesia (20.6% to 15.5%), and China (18.3% to 7.9%), and internal leverage has been reduced from being short 42% cash to being net long 2% cash (Figure 4). The reduction in portfolio leverage makes sense, as higher interest rates mean 'cash' is no longer 'free'.

Sector-wise, the FAX fund is still predominantly invested in government bonds, although exposures have been reduced across the board due to reduced leverage (Figure 5).

Easing Of Interest Rates Have Boosted Short-Term Returns

Regarding returns, the brutal bond bear market of 2022 partly reversed in 2023, allowing the FAX fund to deliver 10.6% in total returns on an NAV basis for the year, the highest since 2019 (Figure 6). YTD, the FAX fund has returned an additional 5.9% to October 15, 2024.

FAX's improved performance can be attributed to the easing of interest rates, as inflation appears to have stabilized and global central banks are now on a rate-easing path. For example, the Indian 10-year treasury yield peaked at 7.5% in early 2022 and has since moderated to 6.8% (Figure 7).

However, compared to peers in the Emerging Markets Bond category on Morningstar, FAX's recent returns have been poor, as both 2023 and YTD returns are ranked fourth quartile.

Unfortunately, FAX's modest rebound since 2023 has not been able to boost the fund's long-term returns, leaving the fund with 3/5/10/15Yr average annual returns of -2.2%/-0.1%/0.8%/2.6% respectively to September 30, 2024. The FAX fund is ranked either 3rd or 4th quartile on all timeframes.

Distributions Have Been Funded For Now...

FAX's modest performance in 2023 has been able to fund its distribution. In the fiscal year to October 31, 2023, the FAX fund generated $0.31 / share in investment income and realized gains, which was roughly sufficient to fund its $0.33 / share distribution (Figure 8).

With the reverse stock split, FAX's monthly distribution rises from $0.0275 / share to $0.165 / share, or a 10.8% distribution yield on its most recent net asset value ("NAV") of $18.31 / share.

However, on a longer-term timeframe, I am still concerned about the fund's ability to generate enough income to fund its distribution. For example, in fiscal years 2019 to 2023, the FAX fund generated a net investment loss of $0.07 / share but paid out $1.69 / share in distributions. This overpayment of distributions relative to earnings power depletes the FAX fund's NAV.

Over the past decade, the FAX fund's NAV and market price have declined by 54% and 52% respectively due to poor investment performance and the overpayment of distributions (Figure 9).

Although FAX's NAV was able to hold steady in the past 2 years, the long-term trend is still down.

Heavily Biased To Easing Cycle

Looking forward, unless the FAX fund can raise its average annual returns to ~10%+, I do not foresee the fund's distribution rate as sustainable.

In particular, if we look at 10-year treasury yields across the Asia-Pacific, with a few exceptions like India and Indonesia (which are FAX's largest country exposures), most nations have treasury yields ranging from 2-5% (Figure 10).

This means that for the FAX fund to achieve its ~10% return hurdle, it will most likely have to rely heavily on realized gains from interest rate declines. This is not a long-term sustainable strategy since there is a lower bound to interest rates.

Discount Narrows On Modest Rebound In Performance

With the modest rebound in performance in 2023 and 2024, investor confidence has returned to the FAX fund and its discount to NAV has been reduced from ~15% in May 2023 to 7% currently (Figure 11).

This is near the lowest discount to NAV on the FAX fund in the past decade.

Conclusion

The abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund Inc has rebounded since the last time I reviewed the fund, returning 10.6% in 2023 and 5.9% YTD in 2024. This has allowed the FAX fund to fund its generous distribution yield, currently set at 10.8% of NAV.

Although the fund has performed well in the past year, I still have concerns about the long-term sustainability of the FAX fund's distribution. Looking forward, the yields in its portfolio do not appear sufficient to fund its distribution yield. This means the FAX fund will have to rely heavily on trading gains to generate income. This is not a long-term sustainable strategy, since there is a lower bound to interest rates.

I continue to be cautious about the FAX fund and would recommend investors seek yield elsewhere. For example, North American investment-grade bonds are yielding ~5% unlevered and investors willing to take on modest leverage and credit risk may be able to achieve even higher returns (Figure 12).

Why hold Asian geopolitical exposure when decent returns can be achieved with safer North American investment-grade corporate bonds?

data.title: Cryptocurrencies: Bitcoin Climbs Above $60K
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

This weekly update tracks some of the largest cryptocurrencies by market share: bitcoin and ether. While both are considered to be high risk when it comes to investing, the two have foundational differences that investors should know.

We've also included XRP, as it was one of the largest cryptocurrencies when this series began. According to Wikipedia, a cryptocurrency is "a digital asset designed to work as a medium of exchange that uses cryptography to secure its transactions, to control the creation of additional units, and to verify the transfer of assets."

Bitcoin

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) was the world's first cryptocurrency and decentralized digital currency. The first bitcoin transaction occurred in early 2009 and has since grown worldwide.

Bitcoin's price climbed above the $60,000 level this past week. BTC is currently up ~52% year-to-date.

Ether

Ether (ETH-USD) is another cryptocurrency run on the Ethereum blockchain platform and has the second-largest market share, despite being the newest of the three discussed in this article, with its launch in July 2015.

Ether's price climbed higher, ending above $2,600. ETH is now up ~11% year-to-date.

XRP

XRP (XRP-USD), which is owned by Ripple and launched in 2012, was one of the larger cryptocurrencies for some time until new coins joined the market. We've included it in this article for reference. XRP is currently down ~14% year-to-date.

Bitcoin vs. Ether vs. XRP

An index has been created in order to chart these three cryptocurrencies together, considering their significantly different pricing histories. Bitcoin tops the chart. In other words, the price of a bitcoin has changed the most out of all three cryptocurrencies.

On January 10th, the SEC approved spot bitcoin ETFs from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB), Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB), Coinshares Valkyrie Bitcoin Fund (BRRR). Here are some of the quick takeaways from the spot bitcoin ETF launch.

On July 23rd, a handful of spot ether ETFs launched from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Ethereum Trust (ETHE), Franklin Ethereum ETF (EZET), Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW), iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA), Fidelity Ethereum ETF (FETH).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Small Caps Compelling For Patient Investors
data.description:
Small Cap Have Not Kept Pace with Large Caps

Over the past few years, the rapid rise of the Magnificent Seven[1] and concentration in the S&P 500 Index have dominated the headlines and, understandably, garnered significant investor interest. However, as this concentration of AI beneficiaries has absorbed market attention and investors’ capital, it has often come at the expense of small-cap stocks. In fact, the large cap Russell 1000 Index has outperformed the small-cap Russell 2000 Index in nine of the past 10 years, with large caps returning approximately 106 percentage points more than small caps. Despite a sharp rally for small caps in July, it appears that 2024 is poised to follow a similar trajectory, with large caps extending their outperformance by 8.4 percentage points through the end of August. While this type of market-cap-based leadership isn’t unprecedented, it has now pushed valuation spreads between small and large cap stocks to historically extreme levels. While it remains difficult to say when this trend will finally break, we believe that history has shown that all market cycles eventually end and that small caps will rotate back into market leadership, to the benefit of patient investors.

A Brief History of Market Capitalization Cycles

Equity markets are replete with market cycles, and the swinging pendulum of market leadership between large and small caps is a perfect example. Since the summer of 1926, we have identified 12 distinct periods of at least three years where market-cap-based leadership was evident (Exhibit 1). This analysis, which is based on Fama-French data for the small-minus-big (SMB) universe, yields two noteworthy observations for small-cap investors: 1) small has led large more than 60% of the time, and 2) the current 10-year run for large is the longest on record. While we believe that predicting the precise timing of such a rotation is a fool’s errand, an eventual return to small-cap leadership has history on its side.

Exhibit 1: Historical Size-Based Leadership Favors Small Caps

Interestingly, today’s large cap leadership has been defined by a very narrow set of the largest large cap companies. From 2013 to 2023, the 10 largest stocks in the Russell 1000 Index nearly doubled in weight, from 14% to 27%, before surging to over 31% at the end of August — a level of market concentration not seen since the early 1960s. More astonishing, however, is the rate of increase in concentration over the last decade, which is the most rapid consolidation since 1950.[2] The annual rebalance of the Russell 2000 Index should have served to insulate small-cap managers from such periods of concentrated performance, but it failed to do so for active managers that didn’t own Super Micro Computer (SMCI) over the last two years, as SMCI reached the highest-ever weighting in the index in March 2024 by more than 100 basis points, for a total weight of 2.13%.

The last decade’s large cap dominance has bled over into the universe of small-cap managers. In fact, according to Lipper Fund performance data, small-cap active managers have migrated toward larger small caps, with the Small Core Lipper peer group carrying a $7.9 billion average market cap at the end of the second quarter of 2024, compared to the Russell 2000 average of $2.6 billion.

Small-Cap Valuations at Extreme Levels

This extended run of large cap dominance has led to extremes, both in terms of relative performance and relative valuations, that the market has not seen in more than two decades. In fact, the Russell 2000 Index’s performance relative to the Russell 1000 Index stands in the bottom decile since 1978, only slightly better than its relative performance during the Dot-Com era of the early 2000s (Exhibit 2). Similarly, relative valuation metrics such as price to book (P/B) and price to earnings (P/E) have also declined to their 2000s levels (Exhibit 3).

Exhibit 2: Small Relative Performance Near Historic Lows

Exhibit 3: Small-Cap Metrics at Multi-Decade Lows

While this divergence between small and large cap performance and relative valuations are telling, they do not support a prediction for when a sustained rotation in market leadership to small cap stocks will occur. Rather, we believe these observations, juxtaposed alongside the pattern of historical size-based leadership cycles, suggest that current market conditions have created an excellent opportunity for investors to consider increasing the small cap allocation in their portfolio.

The Potent Power of Valuation and Quality

While small caps in general will benefit from a cyclical rotation out of large caps, we believe that betting on the size factor is not enough to generate consistent outperformance by active managers. Rather, generating sustained outperformance requires a focus on both valuation discipline as well as a portfolio constructed of high-quality companies.

Quality appears to be the key ingredient, not only for teasing out the size factor, but also for generating excess returns within the small-cap universe. When Eugene Fama and Kenneth French (namesakes of the Fama-French three-factor model) first found that small-cap companies have higher risk-adjusted returns than larger cap companies, it was considered revolutionary and groundbreaking — enough so to be one of the first major challenges to the capital asset pricing model (CAPM). However, since then, this size premium has consistently failed to materialize, casting doubt on both practical implementation and even on its theoretical existence. However, more recent research has shown that a strong and distinct size premium emerges when quality factors are incorporated.[3] This is primarily a function of the disparity in quality between small and large, which is to say that small-cap companies are of generally lower quality than their large cap peers. Additionally, research has shown that the size factor is almost exclusively driven by the small-cap quality. In other words, to truly capitalize on the size factor, investors must also avoid low-quality small caps.[4]

We believe that avoiding these low-quality small caps has become even more paramount given the secular decline in the overall quality level of the Russell 2000 Index since the turn of the century. At present, 44% of the names in the Russell 2000 benchmark are unprofitable over the last 12 months, while the return on invested capital spread of large and small companies has swelled from an average of 80 basis points in the 1990s to 410 basis points by the end of 2023.[5] This suggests that the quality gap between small and large cap stocks is widening, but it is worth asking: is avoiding low-quality small caps enough to generate superior risk-adjusted returns versus the Russell 2000?

ClearBridge’s quantitative research team tested this question and found that a portfolio composed of small-cap stocks with top-two quintile rankings in both value and quality factors not only generates superior risk-adjusted returns, but it also does so without sacrificing absolute upside. Specifically, this high-quality value” (HQV) portfolio has generated a compound annual growth rate of 14.5% compared to the Russell 2000’s 8.5%, with annualized volatility of just 21%. In back testing the HQV portfolio over the last 28 years, the portfolio outperformed the benchmark in 23 years over the period, an 82% success rate. This data suggests that the value factor alone can’t produce these results, but rather that this strategic combination of value and quality not only enhances overall portfolio performance but also lowers volatility, thus achieving superior risk-adjusted returns. Thus, we believe that small cap investors that build a portfolio of stocks with high-value and quality factors can get the best of both worlds.

Exhibit 4: Quality and Value Factors Combine to Outperform

The quantitative testing that produced this result selected publicly traded stocks in 1996 that were constituents of the Russell 3000 Index with market caps below the 20th percentile and a trailing three-month average daily volume above the 25th percentile, in order to create a sample set of stocks that we believe best represents a truly investible U.S. small-cap universe. Using this sample set, the team constructed six portfolios based on our proprietary quality and value factor models (Exhibit 5). The portfolios were then tested with both equal- and value-weighted structures, which showed little impact on overall results.

The results of the test showed that not only did the HQV portfolio outperform the Russell 2000 Index, but that is also outperformed each of the other five portfolios on both an absolute and risk-adjusted bases over the 28-year period (Exhibit 6). Additionally, the results showed similar outperformance over most multiyear periods within the overall 28-year test, suggesting that valuation and quality are a potent combination across various market environments.

Exhibit 5: Portfolios Constructed to Represent a Range of Factors

Exhibit 6: High-Quality Value Proves Dominant

Positioned for Small Value and Quality Outperformance

We believe that the combination of these findings, along with historical precedent on size-based market leadership cycles and the current market extremes in small-cap relative valuation levels, point to a favorable setup for patient small-cap investors. However, we believe that an eventual rotation to small-cap leadership will prove particularly beneficial to the ClearBridge Small-Cap Strategy, as our rigorous investment process favors high-quality companies trading at a significant discount to their intrinsic business value. This results in a portfolio that maintains a generally positive tilt to both quality and value factors relative to our Russell 2000 benchmark, which, we believe, will provide added tailwinds when this long-anticipated rotation finally occurs.

Erich Frey, CFA, is a Portfolio Analyst at ClearBridge Investments and has over 19 years of investment experience. Erich earned his BS in Business Administration and Psychology from Washington and Lee University and is a member of the CFA Institute

[1] Alphabet, Apple, Amazon.com, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Nvidia and Tesla.

[2] Elroy Dimson, Paul Marsh, and Mike Staunton, Triumph of the Optimist: 101 Years of Global investment Returns (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 2002), 28-32.

[3] Asness, Clifford, et al. “Size matters, if you control your junk.” Journal of Financial Economics, vol. 129, no. 3, Sept. 2018, pp. 479–509.

[4] Blitz, David and Hanauer, Matthias Xaver, Settling the Size Matter (September 4, 2020).

[5] Mauboussin, Michael and Callahan, Dan, “Stock Market Concentration: How Much Is Too Much?” Counterpoint Global Insights, Morgan Stanley Investment Management, 4 June 2024.

data.title: Netflix Earnings Preview: Advertising Expected To Drive Revenue Growth For Next Few Years
data.description:
On Thursday night, October 17th, Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) will release their 3rd quarter ’24 financial results after the closing bell.

With the stock’s valuation, it’s always a “heart attack on a plate” waiting for Netflix’s numbers, but the streaming giant has continued to deliver for shareholders, and the important technical metric is, in the last month, Netflix has traded over $700, or its 11/19/21 high print of $700, and the stock has remained above that key level. That being said, it hasn’t been a robust “breakout” but more of a lackluster move above the Nov ’21 peak.

It’s rare for a fundamental analyst to start an earnings preview off with a chart, but here’s Netflix’s weekly chart:

Speaking of technicals, the S&P 500’s growth stock leadership of the last few years hasn’t traded well since late June, early July, ’24, lagging the S&P 500 and some other asset classes in the 3rd quarter ’24, and the fact is Netflix is part of that crowd. It’s not part of the “Mega-cap 7” so to speak, but Netflix is valued like those names, and thus subject to the bottom falling out of bed on a numbers miss.

Fundamental and valuation metrics

When Netflix reports their results Thursday night, after the bell, consensus sell-side estimates will be expecting $5.12 in EPS on $9.77 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth of 37% and 14% respectively. The consensus operating income estimate is $2.72 billion and is expected to be up 37% y-o-y.

In the June ’24 quarter, NFLX reported 17% revenue growth, 38% operating income growth, and 48% EPS growth, all y-o-y.

There is no question, the biggest improvement in Netflix’s fundamentals in the last 3 years has been the improvement in operating cash flow and free cash flow.

In the Sept ’22 quarter, NFLX’s trailing twelve month (TTM) free cash flow was just $533 million, and in the subsequent 7 quarters since then has risen to $6.8 billion as of the June ’24 quarter. Consensus estimate for free cash flow per LSEG for Q3 ’24 is $1.7 billion.

LSEG’s free cash flow estimate for calendar ’24 is $6.4 billion, and they could be past that at $6.6 billion by the 3rd quarter, depending on Thursday night’s release. Morningstar has a $10 billion free cash estimate for Netflix by the end of 2028.

As of today’s price, Netflix sports a 2.2% free cash flow yield, which is unusual for a growth stock trading at 37x ’24 EPS.

Netflix is trading at 37x expected ’24 EPS of $19.13, which is 60% growth over 2023’s actual EPS of $11.99. Looking out using ’24 to ’26 EPS averages and P/E averages, the stock is trading at 33x three-year average EPS for expected 31% growth, so on a “P/E to growth” basis, there are some doubters on the expected EPS growth.

To round out the valuation metrics, NFLX is trading at 7x price to sales, and 29x and 41x price to cash flow and price to free cash flow.

Here’s a quick look at EPS and revenue estimate revisions for Netflix:

EPS

That’s an 18-month look for Netflix. That’s a 31% increase in the EPS estimate from 6/30/23 through 10/16/24 for 2024 and then a 27% increase for the same period for 2025. Roughly 8 quarters ago, estimates were looking for high 20% EPS growth for 2024; today, the expected EPS growth for NFLX for 2024 is 60%.

Revenue

The revenue estimate revisions are much smaller for Netflix, although analysts probably understand this as the US streaming market is reaching some saturation (thought US and Canada is 60%, which still seems like some room for growth to me), and Netflix’s member growth will come from other geographies like Southeast Asia, India, South America, and maybe a little more in Europe and Canada.

The Next Big Thing

On August 20th, 2024, Variety Magazine released a story that noted that Netflix’s “upfront advertising” had doubled, and then the company came out and confirmed with this (cut-and-pasted from Briefing.com)

Here’s what followed from the sell-side analysts after this advertising update:

  • 8/21/24: TD Cowen analyst sees ad revenue growing from 4% to 13% by ’29
  • 8/30/24: Pivotal Research boosts NFLX price target (PT) to $900
  • 9/13/24: JPMorgan analyst Doug Anmuth says NFLX ad tier revenue to reach 10% of total rev by ’27
  • 10/1/24: KeyBanc analyst raised NFLX PT to $760
  • 10/7/24: Barclays downgrades NFLX to underweight
  • 10/7/24: Piper Sandler upgrades NFLX to overweight
  • 10/10/24: Oppenheimer upgrades NFLX PT to $775
  • 10/11/24: Guggenheim upgrades NFLX PT to $810
  • 10/16/24: Loop Capital upgrades NFLX PT to $800

Bet that Barclays analyst feels lonely.

That incremental ad revenue, while small today, is likely revenue with a greater margin than the traditional streaming business.

Morningstar has a 30% operating margin target on NFLX by the end of 2028, (NFLX’s operating margin was 26% in the June ’24 quarter and 28% in the March ’24 quarter).

Finally, per some of the sell-side comments, NFLX is due to hit subscribers with another subscription price increase, supposedly before year-end ’24.

Summary/conclusion

There is a lot of nervousness around Netflix’s earnings coming into Thursday night, but that’s always the case given the valuation. If readers cannot handle a pullback in the stock to $600 or even $500, then be sure and adjust your risk coming into earnings.

I am less nervous after writing this earnings preview given the above commentary and results around the advertising business or “ad tier” as sell-side analysts call it.

It’s clear that NFLX’s advertising “flywheel” so to speak, might lead to additional revenue opportunities, but what those might be aren’t entirely clear yet. The two NFL football games that will be offered on Christmas Day could be an additional for Netflix given the 4th quarter every year is typically NFLX’s weakest quarter.

Given how growth stocks are trading, the thought was to trim some NFLX prior to the release, but now, expect positions to be held as it is coming into Thursday night’s earnings release, even though the stock could easily trade off $50 after release.

Longer term, it’s clear that NFLX’s business model is just crushing the competition. When Bob Iger of Disney (DIS) came out in the last 3-4 months, and openly paid homage to NFLX’s dominance in streaming, it caught my attention. Tough competitors usually don’t do that.

Could Netflix be putting in a big double-top on the weekly chart, shown above? Absolutely. Know your risk.

The EPS and revenue estimate revisions should give readers at least a little comfort around sell-side expectations.

Here are some articles written on Netflix in previous quarters, both an earnings preview and then a follow-up: July ’24 here and here, April ’24 here and here. And there’s more on this blog’s website.

Netflix was up 45% YTD after Tuesday night's October 15th, 2024, market close, a little less than 2x the S&P 500’s YTD return.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All EPS and revenue estimates are sourced from LSEG. Readers should gauge their own comfort level with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly. Investing can involve the loss of principal, even on a short-term basis.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Are Investors Offered A Rare Entry Point Into ASML After Earnings?
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) identifies itself for investors as the "most important tech company you've never heard of". Indeed, ASML does not share the fame and trendiness of rock star Nvidia, or of other big names in the chips industry such as TSMC, Broadcom, AMD and Intel. But ASML is as important, it is a hidden gem sitting back upstream at a critical part of the value chain of the semiconductor chips industry.

Critical niche and moat-protected business model

ASML was founded in the Netherlands in 1984 - when modern-day technology was in infant stages, and when AI was more reserved for sci-fi futuristic movies. The company has grown to employ 42,000 employees today in more than 60 locations. Last year, the company generated EUR 28 billion in turnover, with a gross margin of 51%, leaving it with a generous EUR 14 billion to invest EUR 4 billion in R&D and in strengthening its niche and expanding its growth. Revenues increased by 130% in the past five years.

ASML has a niche in lithography systems, which use laser light to print patters of microchips. The systems that ASML produces are a mix of hardware and software innovations that are unique to the company. ASML then supplies these systems to semiconductor manufacturers such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, which produces standardized foundry chips for other companies down the value chain, such as Nvidia, AMD and Qualcomm. ASML has an effective monopoly in producing lithography systems that can manufacture chips smaller than 7nm node, which allows chip manufactures to produce smaller and faster chips.

With 10,000 customers, ASML covers a wide range of industries that use semiconductors, ranging from fast-moving industries related to AI and cloud computing to slower-moving industries such as telecoms, healthcare, automotive and consumer electronics.

Recent results tempered the optimism

This week, the share price of ASML dropped by around 20% in 2 days - so far. The shares now sit where they were at the beginning of the year. The stampede of exit by investors was prompted by an unplanned early announcement of Q3 results that were pretty gloomy - defying all the recent hype about AI's boosting impact on the semiconductor industry. In its announcement, ASML estimated that 2024 full-year sales would be a standstill compared to 2023, with only a minor uplift. 2025 is forecasted by the company to have another minor uplift, with an increase in sales of only 7% on the lower side of the range. The company also reported that gross margins will be subdued - albeit remaining in the 50% range - as the market is subdued and customers are "cautious". New customer orders came in around half expectations, and half the rate of earlier in the year. Adding to that; recent half-year sales dropped by 16%, net income by 18% and operating income margin by 2%.

Even with a 20% drop, the current share price of EUR 650 per share leaves the shares trading at 32x, 2023 P/E. This is a modest valuation for the neck-breaking speed growth that the company was expected to experience. The share price dropped by 37% from the all-time high that was reached only 3 months ago, and is now sitting 18% higher than the 52-week low set exactly 1 year ago in October 2023. The median analyst estimates of share price is EUR 960 per share - providing a 44% upside.

But the recent Q3 results announcement put a harsh brake on expectations of lofty growth. A 30x P/E is definitely not high if growth is in the double-digits range. But if growth reaches a standstill or is in single digits, then there could be concerns that a hefty valuation premium does not have legs. Investors are justified to be cautious; even with the 20% drop, a 32x P/E is still on the high side.

Demand risks

China accounted for around 40% of sales in the past year. Definite slowdown of the Chinese economy and of consumer purchasing power, combined with Western restrictions on China's access to semiconductor technology, are clear contributing factors behind the slowdown of business for ASML.

Adding to that; aside from AI and advanced computing-related sectors, there seems to be a wider slowdown in demand for semiconductor, which could be signs for a wider economic slowdown - not only in China but elsewhere as well.

Fundamentally solid investment, but caution warranted about timing of entry

ASML, without doubt, has a solid business model, that is well-protected by a strong technological and customer-reach moat. The company's financial metrics are very solid; solid revenue generation, superior profitability, and a very strong balance sheet underpinned by an A+ credit rating from Standard and Poor's. ASML is not a high-risk investment. But ASML does not operate in a vacuum; if its industry slows down, so will its growth. Whether the current valuation represents the right entry point, or whether ASML's share price has more to shed, this remains to be seen. For existing investors; there is no need to panic or to consider selling for a loss. For fresh investors, close monitoring is essential to pick the right entry point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
